overview.executiveSummary: Richest data week ever — 80,827 words across 43 enriched wiki pages. Full speaker-attributed transcripts reveal what summaries hid: specific commitments with names attached, verbatim mentor advice worth thousands in consulting fees, and a career-defining tension between startup ambition and family obligation that your friend articulated at 3 AM. The HSIL Hackathon wasn't just a win — it was a 69-conversation masterclass in healthcare commercialization. Three things demand action before April 23: (1) Return flight to California for USCIS biometrics; (2) NORA deliverables before Harvard cuts 50% of teams; (3) Converting the 10+ startup mentor relationships into a durable network before the hackathon glow fades. The transcripts also reveal you're carrying an unresolved tension: multiple people are telling you to narrow focus, but you keep adding projects (NORA + Shoei + VR curriculum + AI Nutrition + NOURISH + CGM). The mentors' own words — 'narrow it down, pick ONE problem' — apply to you too.

crossSourceAlignment.statusLabel: OMI-only — needs Airtable epic + business plan NOW
Manuscript writing stalled, data analysis done
Google.org Apr 17, PCORI LOI Apr 28 — both imminent

overview.redFlag: The transcripts reveal a pattern the summaries masked: you committed to weekly 1-hour NORA team meetings, promised to review a friend's hackathon presentation, told Jennifer you'd formalize her role, and verbally agreed to increase Dalia's hours — all in the same 48 hours. Zero calendar invites created, zero emails sent. The full transcripts show this isn't forgetfulness — it's commitment overload. The mentors you admired this week all said the same thing: 'narrow it down.'

omi.note: ENRICHED EXTRACTION: 43 wiki pages now contain full speaker-attributed transcripts (80,827 words, up from 22,705 summary-only). This reveals specific quotes, exact commitments, named individuals, and relationship dynamics invisible in summaries.
newInsightsFromTranscripts: Mentor's exact commercialization advice captured verbatim — provisional patent strategy, PCB miniaturization pathway, co-manufacturing with Indian brands (Noise)
Friend's 3 AM career confession: '$100M valuation → exit with $10M → ETFs → passive income.' Parents aging, girlfriend tension, US vs UK decision.

Mental health AI team pivoted mid-session from student monitoring to AI-camera behavioral detection — faculty forced the pivot with legal constraint: 'AI can never prescribe. If even a medicine name appears, you'll go to jail.'

Therapist interview revealed hard clinical boundary on AI: uses AI for marketing/admin but 'truly senior millennial' on clinical note-writing. Tried giving 10 therapists free licenses — most quit within a week.
Faculty on VR effectiveness: 'If I give a slide deck about systolic heart failure, they'll doze off because they're tired. VR forces full focus — no option to think about other things.'
Deloitte mentor who left for Medibank: 'I feel like this is all about making money, and there's no impact being made in healthcare.' Developed 'ecosystem thinking' framework before departing.
Budget granularity: Dalia at $16/hr × 3hrs/week, $9 recurring for CGM, $2,500 study personnel allocation, PFME budget supplements manuscript costs. Eli confirmed breathing room.

Post-hackathon LinkedIn strategy: three-post plan with specific angles (mentoring, institutional gratitude, peer congratulations). Photo editing needed to remove Gucci belt — brand consistency concern.

themes.title: Hackathon as Commercialization Bootcamp
themes.detail: 69+ conversations from HSIL. Full transcripts reveal Stefan didn't just compete — he absorbed a masterclass in healthcare business models from 3+ senior mentors across cardiac devices, oncology AI, neonatal monitoring, hospital waste, drug supply chains, and women's health. Specific revenue models, margin targets, and market sizing captured verbatim.

themes.title: The Narrowing Imperative
themes.detail: Every mentor this week said some version of 'narrow it down.' Cardiac team told: focus on triage, not echo replacement. Blood app told: saturate blood before adding pharmacy. Women's health told: pick ONE life stage. This advice applies directly to Stefan's own project portfolio.

themes.title: VR Education — From Theory to Curriculum
themes.detail: Four-module curriculum structure designed (trust-building → behavior change sustenance). Faculty quote: 'VR forces full focus.' IRB demo needed by ~Apr 30. Collaborator Minal Moharir central. $5K PFME grant for culturally tailored nutrition education.

themes.title: Career Crossroads Conversation (3 AM)
themes.detail: Deep late-night debate: startup vs corporate, US vs UK, relationship tension, financial independence model ($100M→exit→ETFs). SPEAKER_6 delivered aggressive confidence coaching: 'Self belief and execution matter more than raw IQ... staying in a routine is the most risky in terms of wasted potential.'

themes.title: India Healthcare Market Intelligence
themes.detail: State procurement data captured: Odisha ₹1,000 crores/yr, Karnataka ₹19-20,000 crores, Tamil Nadu ₹31,000 crores. Government stores have minimal audits = opportunity. ASHA workers as primary distribution for pediatric/antenatal drugs. Private chains (Apollo, Tata 1mg) too efficient to disrupt.

ideas.pgQuoteTop: The way to get startup ideas is not to try to think of startup ideas.
ideas.pgQuoteBottom: If you can just avoid dying, you get rich.

ideas.title: NORA — From Hackathon Trophy to Harvard Pipeline
ideas.subtitle: The Clock Is Ticking on 1st Place
ideas.edge: 1st place + Harvard boot camp + Western Sydney LHD CTI approval
ideas.essay: The transcript of the post-win conversation reveals something the summary missed: your team knows exactly what Harvard wants but hasn't started any of it. SPEAKER_4 spelled it out verbatim: 'They wanna see some motion — you've reached out to people, you're trying to get letters of intent, you've figured out a basic business plan.' The team response? 'Our group don't have engineers. If they ask to build a final product, we're fucked.' The real move: use ATBI's complimentary incubation offer for engineering support, leverage the Western Sydney LHD CTI approval (SPEAKER_3: 'We literally got the approval from the CTI'), and set the weekly meeting you promised but never calendared. Harvard cuts 50% of teams. Motion, not perfection.

ideas.title: Shoei — The Data Layer Disguised as Footwear
ideas.subtitle: Hospital Infection Intelligence via Smart Shoes
ideas.edge: Infection control market + Vietnam manufacturing + no direct competitors
ideas.essay: Two separate conversations this week converged on hospital infection control as an underserved data layer. The Teal conversation explored AI + SME expertise for ~10% infection rate reduction. The innovation observation: the product isn't the shoe — it's traffic heat maps, EVS timing optimization, and cleaning verification data. Hospitals will pay for infection intelligence because even small reductions in HAIs save millions in liability. The manufacturing insight (Vietnam-based, cheap components) makes hardware viable. Nobody else is combining wearables + infection control + hospital operations intelligence. You explicitly requested a pitch deck be built — now build it.

ideas.title: Pneumatic Hospital Waste Intelligence
ideas.subtitle: Japanese Tech Meets Indian Hospital Chaos
ideas.edge: Direct from mentor expertise at hackathon
seeds: Hackathon mentoring session — waste segregation team (Apr 11)
ideas.essay: A team pitched automated waste bins and the mentor demolished it — then rebuilt it better. The transcript captured the exact pivot: 'Segregation is already done efficiently in good hospitals. The real use case is trauma/ICU.' Then the golden reference: pneumatic chute systems already work in Japanese and Swedish hospitals — multi-floor, minimal infection risk, reduced cleaning workload. The mentor said: 'Have a look at models already there.' The business case: frame around infection reduction + cleaning workload reduction for hospital CEOs (not technicians). This isn't about bins — it's about pneumatic infrastructure intelligence for Indian hospitals.

ideas.title: ASHA Worker Drug Expiry Guardian
ideas.subtitle: ₹31,000 Crore Market Nobody's Watching
ideas.edge: State procurement data from mentor + government audit gap
ideas.essay: The mentor dropped market intelligence that would cost ₹50 lakhs from a consulting firm. Verbatim: 'State procurement of Odisha = 1,000 crores. Karnataka = 19-20,000 crores. Tamil Nadu = 31,000 crores.' Private chains (Apollo, Tata 1mg) are too efficient to disrupt — but government stores have minimal audits. The narrow play: expiry tracking for pediatric/antenatal drugs delivered via ASHA workers. Iron, ferritin, D3, baby vaccines — shelf bottles under ₹100 with 6-month shelf life, sunlight-sensitive. One subscription to state governments for drug expiry alerts = recurring revenue at scale.

ideas.title: AI Camera Behavioral Detection in Schools
ideas.subtitle: The Pivot That Saved a Mental Health Startup
ideas.edge: Live pivot captured in transcript — faculty forced the redesign
seeds: Faculty mentoring session — mental health AI team (Apr 11)
Legal constraint: AI cannot prescribe in India
ideas.essay: The transcript captured a real-time conceptual pivot. Student team pitched student mental health monitoring. Faculty demolished it: 'Even if your parameter is perfect, even if you can predict, then what next? Is a social worker gonna come to his house?' Then rebuilt it: AI cameras in public spaces (classrooms, hostel corridors) detecting non-verbal stress indicators — rubbing ears, aloofness, restlessness. Alert goes to psychiatrist with 15-day behavioral clips. The legal boundary was explicit: 'AI can never prescribe. If even a medicine name appears, you'll go to jail.' The research angle: validate which behavioral indicators predict crisis across Indian student populations.

ideas.title: VR Forces Focus — The Faculty Insight
ideas.subtitle: Why Slide Decks Fail and VR Doesn't
ideas.edge: Faculty quote + 4-module curriculum designed this week
ideas.essay: Faculty said it plainly: 'If I give a slide deck about systolic heart failure, they'll doze off because they're tired. VR forces full focus — no option to think about other things.' This week you designed a four-module VR curriculum: (1) Trust-building → (2) Behavior change → (3) Resistance → (4) Sustenance. The example scenario: Patient Mrs. Priya says 'my health numbers aren't improving, I feel like a failure' — resident must interrupt default lab-data reflex and address emotional context first. The publishable insight: VR's advantage isn't immersion — it's attentional capture in a population (residents) with chronic fatigue.

ideas.title: Oncology Patient Journey Compression
ideas.subtitle: From 30 Days to 2 Days — The Specimen Problem
ideas.edge: Personal oncology experience shared by mentor in transcript
ideas.essay: A mentor shared his own family's cancer experience in the transcript. The delay was staggering: 'The moment I went to CMC Vellore, the doctor said interpretation seems off. Can I get the cell sample which went to Delhi? It took six days. Otherwise, I'll have to operate again.' Then the vision: 'If you have AI telling the patient within three days he can get that sample — time reduced from one month to two days.' The research question: What percentage of oncology treatment delays are caused by specimen logistics vs. clinical decision-making? If it's specimen logistics (slides, tissue blocks, reports), AI routing + specialist matching could save thousands of lives.

ideas.title: The Narrowing Imperative
ideas.subtitle: Every Mentor Said the Same Thing
ideas.edge: Pattern extracted from 10+ mentoring sessions this week
ideas.essay: Across 10+ startup mentoring sessions this week, every mentor delivered the same verdict using different words. Blood app: 'Narrow to blood as priority 1 — saturate completely before adding pharmacy.' Women's health: 'Which segment? Puberty? Pregnancy? Pick one.' ICU platform: 'Don't compare with Epic — target tier 2-3 hospitals.' Cardiac device: 'Focus on triage, not echo replacement.' The meta-insight: the teams that narrowed fastest won the most mentor enthusiasm. The ones that tried to solve everything got the harshest feedback. This applies directly to Stefan's own portfolio. Write it as a LinkedIn essay: 'What 10 Healthcare Mentors Taught Me in 72 Hours.'

ideas.title: The Limiting Mindset in Australian Startups
ideas.subtitle: Why Sales People Make Better Founders
ideas.edge: Direct quote from post-hackathon career debate
seeds: Australia vs US startup culture comparison
ideas.essay: The 3 AM career debate produced a crystallized insight. SPEAKER_4: 'Most of the time, it's a limiting mindset because if you live your whole life in a career sort of workplace, you don't get that much rejection. People in sales tend to do quite well in startups because they're just used to rejection.' SPEAKER_3 added: 'Maybe that's why Australia struggles so much in startups. A limiting mindset.' Then SPEAKER_6 delivered the punchline: 'People overestimate risk and underestimate their ability to return to safe corporate roles if a startup fails.' Write this as: 'Why Australian Healthcare Needs More Salespeople — And Fewer MBAs.'

ideas.title: Indian State Pharma Procurement Map
ideas.subtitle: ₹31,000 Crores That Nobody's Tracking
ideas.essay: The mentor rattled off state procurement figures that should be a visualization. Odisha: ₹1,000 crores/year. Karnataka: ₹19-20,000 crores. Tamil Nadu: ₹31,000 crores. Map each Indian state by annual pharma procurement spending, overlay with: (a) audit frequency, (b) drug expiry rates, (c) ASHA worker density. The government stores with minimal audits are where medicines expire unnoticed — and where babies receive expired vaccines. This map is the pitch deck slide that gets a government contract.

ideas.title: Duolingo for Cooking — The Sous Consumer Layer
ideas.subtitle: Micro-Lessons + Agentic AI + Fridge Photos
ideas.essay: A friend brainstormed a consumer cooking app during the trip: micro-lessons taught by chefs teaching professional cooking progression, with agentic AI at every layer. Features discussed: recipe saving, AI that pulls trending TikTok dishes, analyzes fridge photos to suggest meals, learns taste preferences. B2C SaaS model. The connection to Sous: this IS the consumer engagement layer for your research platform. The research backend generates clinically validated cultural recipes. The Duolingo layer makes them addictive. The monetization discussion got dark (selling data to McDonald's) — but the core product is sound.

ideas.title: The Ecosystem Thinking Framework
ideas.subtitle: Stolen from a Disillusioned Medibank Executive
ideas.edge: Deloitte mentor's developed framework + healthcare application
ideas.essay: In the post-hackathon conversation, a friend described their former Deloitte mentor who left for Medibank and became disillusioned: 'She was like, I feel like this is all about making money, and there's no impact being made in healthcare.' Before leaving, she developed an 'ecosystem thinking' framework for healthcare consulting. The friend recognized its value: 'I actually think it's an extremely helpful framework to take to healthcare as an industry, as a consultant.' This framework — thinking of healthcare as an interconnected ecosystem rather than isolated service lines — could become a published methodology. Ask the friend for an introduction. This mentor is looking for impact projects.

dataSources.name: Granola
dataSources.desc: Recurring meeting topics, repeated action items, process descriptions

dataSources.name: Airtable
dataSources.desc: Tasks with high 'Times Delayed', recurring patterns, work stream bottlenecks

dataSources.name: OMI
dataSources.desc: Voice notes: 'every week I...', 'I always have to...', delegation intent

dataSources.name: Notion
dataSources.desc: Process docs, SOPs, templates, repeated page structures

candidates.name: OMI Commitment Capture → Airtable
candidates.pattern: 149 conversations = 8+ unactioned verbal commitments identified. At 80K words, manual review is impossible. Full transcripts make extraction feasible.

candidates.name: Grant Deadline Tracker + Pre-Fill
candidates.pattern: 23 grants tracked in registry. 3 deadlines in next 2 weeks (Google.org Apr 17, PCORI ×2 Apr 28). Common sections across applications could be pre-filled.

candidates.name: Hackathon/Conference Follow-Up Automation
candidates.pattern: HSIL generated 50+ contacts, 10+ mentored teams, 3 LinkedIn posts. Currently all manual. Automate: extract names → draft messages → schedule.

candidates.name: Meeting Action Item Extraction
candidates.pattern: After every meeting, 3-5 action items discussed but only ~40% reach Airtable.

candidates.name: Weekly Status Report Generation
candidates.pattern: Manual synthesis from multiple sources. Optimus partially automates.

candidates.name: CGM Participant Follow-Up Templating
candidates.pattern: CGM participant comms follow same structure.

candidates.name: Notion Decision Record Writer
candidates.pattern: Key decisions in meetings rarely documented.

crossSourcePatterns.title: Voice → Task Capture (Critical)
crossSourcePatterns.desc: 80,827 words of enriched transcripts = 100+ potential commitments. 8+ confirmed unactioned. Full transcripts make automated extraction feasible for the first time.
crossSourcePatterns.flow: OMI voice → enriched wiki → skill extraction → Airtable task

crossSourcePatterns.title: Grant Cycle Acceleration
crossSourcePatterns.desc: 23 grants tracked, 3 deadlines in 2 weeks. Common narrative sections (team, preliminary data, institution) could be pre-filled from a master template.

crossSourcePatterns.title: Conference → Follow-Up Pipeline
crossSourcePatterns.desc: HSIL generated massive contact/idea backlog. No system tracks follow-ups, LinkedIn connections, or mentee progress.

crossSourcePatterns.title: Meeting → Task → Doc Pipeline
crossSourcePatterns.desc: Granola meetings generate action items → become Airtable tasks → need Notion docs. Manual at every step.

grants.name: Google.org AI Opportunity Fund — DEADLINE APR 17
grants.match: Sous AI pipeline direct fit. $500K–$3M + Google Cloud credits. AI for social impact mandate. 3 DAYS LEFT.

grants.name: PCORI — Novel Methods for Behavior Change
grants.match: VR + CGM + cultural nutrition = novel methods. LOI in 2 weeks.

grants.name: PCORI — Engagement in Cooking Interventions
grants.match: NOURISH community cooking program. 30,000+ participants. LOI in 2 weeks.

grants.name: NIH/NIDA — PGx Copilot
grants.match: Pharmacogenomics copilot. Aligned with precision medicine direction.

grants.name: AHRQ R21/R33 — Healthcare from the Eye
grants.match: Top-scored grant in registry. Sous, PGx Copilot, Healthcare from the Eye all eligible.

grants.name: NIH R21 — Dietary Patterns & CGM in Diverse Populations
grants.match: Direct fit: CGM + nutrition intervention in underserved communities. 60-person pilot RCT strengthens application.

grants.name: PCORI — Patient-Centered Outcomes (Primary Target: $600K)
grants.match: Primary NOURISH target. VR nutrition + CGM + culturally tailored care = core PCORI mandate. 30,000+ participants. Minal Moharir + Isabela Moise as co-investigators.

grants.name: CHPR Symposium Presentation
grants.match: Network: Lucas William Lowe, Kate McKinney, Josh, Tia, Yasaman, Yetsa. Critical for Stanford positioning.

ideas.name: SpotDx
ideas.tagline: Operation the board game, but teaches medicine.
ideas.mechanic: Click signs on a cartoon patient, classify normal/abnormal, progress through levels.
ideas.researchAngle: Which clinical signs are systematically missed by med students? (publishable)
ideas.claudePrompt: Build a Next.js 15 app named 'spotdx'. Single screen: cartoon patient SVG with 5 clinical signs overlaid at anatomical positions. User clicks each sign, then a modal asks 'Normal or Abnormal?'. Track clicks in PostHog. Deploy to Vercel. MVP signs: jaundice, clubbing, spider nevi, oedema, pallor. Cartoony art style (like Operation board game). Mobile-first.
